jundab-bin-abdullah: Jundab bin Abdullah al-Bajali, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who narrated hadiths and was known for his piety and strict adherence to the Sunnah. He settled in Kufa after the Prophet's death and was among those who transmitted knowledge to the next generation of Muslims. He is particularly known for narrating hadiths related to sincerity of intention and avoiding ostentation in worship.
ibn-abbas: Abdullah ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the Prophet's uncle, making him the Prophet's cousin. He is one of the most prolific narrators of hadith and is widely regarded as the greatest scholar of Quranic exegesis among the companions. The Prophet supplicated for him, saying 'O Allah, grant him understanding of the religion and teach him interpretation.' He narrated thousands of hadiths and founded a major school of Quranic commentary in Mecca.
amir-bin-sad-bin-abi-waqqas: Amir bin Sa'd bin Abi Waqqas was a prominent Tabi'i (successor to the companions) and the son of the illustrious companion Sa'd bin Abi Waqqas, may Allah be pleased with him, one of the ten promised Paradise. Amir narrated extensively from his father and other companions, transmitting a significant body of hadith literature to subsequent generations. He was known as a reliable and trustworthy narrator in the chains of transmission.
abu-al-aswad: Abu Al-Aswad al-Du'ali was a distinguished early scholar of Arabic grammar and a companion or senior Tabi'i who narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with him. He is credited as one of the founders of Arabic grammatical science and contributed significantly to the preservation of the Arabic language. As a hadith narrator, he transmitted important reports from the companions and was considered trustworthy by scholars of hadith criticism.
um-al-fadl: Umm al-Fadl Lubabah bint al-Harith, may Allah be pleased with her, was the wife of al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib and thus the Prophet's aunt by marriage. She was among the earliest women to embrace Islam and is said to have been the second woman to accept the faith after Khadijah. She narrated hadiths from the Prophet and was held in great esteem by the companions for her piety and her close relationship to the Prophet's household.
amra: Amra bint Abd al-Rahman, may Allah have mercy on her, was a leading female scholar of the Tabi'in generation and one of the most important transmitters of hadiths from A'ishah, may Allah be pleased with her. She was regarded by scholars such as Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz as among the most reliable authorities on A'ishah's narrations. Her precision in transmitting knowledge made her a pivotal figure in the preservation of hadith, particularly those concerning women's affairs.
ibn-umar-ra: Abdullah ibn Umar, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of the second caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab and one of the most prolific narrators among the companions, transmitting over two thousand hadiths. He was renowned for his meticulous adherence to the Prophet's Sunnah, following it in the finest detail. He lived a long life after the Prophet's death and was a major source of religious guidance for the Muslim community throughout the early Islamic period.
amr-bin-ash-sharid: Amr bin Ash-Sharid was a companion or early Tabi'i narrator who transmitted hadiths from the Prophet or senior companions. He is recorded in hadith chains relating to various aspects of Islamic practice and was known among scholars of hadith sciences as a narrator whose reports are found in the major collections. Details of his biography are preserved primarily through the hadith chains in which his name appears.
sulaiman-bin-surad: Sulaiman bin Surad al-Khuza'i, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who lived to an advanced age and participated in early Islamic history. He is known for his association with the people of Kufa and is notable in Islamic history for his role in seeking to avenge the death of Husayn ibn Ali. As a hadith narrator, he transmitted reports from the Prophet that are preserved in the major collections.
zainab-bint-jahsh: Zainab bint Jahsh, may Allah be pleased with her, was a wife of the Prophet and one of the Mothers of the Believers. She was the daughter of the Prophet's paternal aunt and was known for her generosity, charity, and skill in handicrafts, using the proceeds of her work to help the poor. She narrated hadiths from the Prophet, including reports related to signs of the Hour and matters of worship. She passed away during the caliphate of Umar ibn al-Khattab.
humaid-bin-abdur-rahman: Humaid bin Abdur-Rahman was a trustworthy Tabi'i narrator who transmitted hadiths from companions including Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with him. He was a jurist from Kufa or Medina whose narrations appear in the major hadith collections. Scholars of hadith sciences classified him as reliable, and his transmissions cover various aspects of Islamic jurisprudence and practice.
ismail-bin-abi-khalid: Isma'il bin Abi Khalid was a highly regarded Tabi'i narrator from Kufa who transmitted hadiths from numerous companions and senior Tabi'in. He was considered one of the most reliable and accurate narrators of his generation, and scholars of hadith praised him extensively for his precision and memory. His narrations appear in all the major hadith collections, covering a wide range of topics in Islamic practice.
qais-bin-ubad: Qais bin Ubad was an early Tabi'i scholar who narrated from companions including Ali ibn Abi Talib and Abu Dharr al-Ghifari, may Allah be pleased with them. He was based in Basra and was known as a reliable transmitter of hadith whose reports are found in the major collections. His narrations contribute importantly to our knowledge of the companions' practice and the early Muslim community.
um-khalid-bint-khalid: Umm Khalid bint Khalid ibn Sa'id, may Allah be pleased with her, was a companion of the Prophet who emigrated to Abyssinia as a child with her father. She had the honor of being dressed by the Prophet himself in a garment brought from Abyssinia, upon which he praised its quality. She lived a long life and narrated hadiths from the Prophet, and her account of being dressed by him is among the well-known narrations in the collections.
ibn-az-zubair: Abdullah ibn az-Zubayr, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of the companion Zubayr ibn al-Awwam and the first child born to the Muhajirun in Medina. He narrated from the Prophet and from numerous companions, and was himself a distinguished companion who fought in several major early battles. He later became the caliph of the Muslim world, ruling from Mecca, and was known for his courage, piety, and devotion to worship.
kab-bin-ujra: Ka'b bin Ujra, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who narrated an important hadith regarding the shaving of the head during ihram when afflicted with illness, including the well-known verses about the fidyah (expiation). He is also associated with the narration of the complete form of the Abrahamic prayer (salawat) recited in the tashahhud. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections.
adi-bin-hatim: Adi bin Hatim al-Ta'i, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of the famous pre-Islamic Arab chieftain Hatim al-Ta'i, renowned for generosity. Adi embraced Islam and became a companion of the Prophet, narrating many hadiths from him. He lived an extraordinarily long life and was among those who remained steadfast during the apostasy wars. He participated in the early Islamic conquests and was a major narrator of the Prophet's teachings.
amr-bin-al-as: Amr bin Al-As, may Allah be pleased with him, was a brilliant military commander and statesman who embraced Islam before the conquest of Mecca and became one of the Prophet's most capable companions. He is celebrated for leading the Islamic conquest of Egypt and serving as its governor. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet covering various aspects of faith and practice, and his narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections.
haritha-bin-wahb: Haritha bin Wahb al-Khuza'i, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet and the maternal brother of Ubaydullah ibn Umar ibn al-Khattab. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet, including well-known reports about the people of Paradise and Hell and the qualities that lead to each. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections and provide valuable insight into the Prophet's teachings on character and the afterlife.
abdur-rahman-bin-samura: Abdur-Rahman bin Samura, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who embraced Islam and participated in the Islamic conquests, serving as a commander in Khorasan. The Prophet gave him important advice regarding leadership and oaths, including the well-known hadith cautioning against seeking positions of authority. His narrations are preserved in the major collections and reflect key aspects of the Prophet's guidance.
anas-bin-malik-rad: Anas bin Malik, may Allah be pleased with him, was the personal servant of the Prophet for ten years, from the time the Prophet arrived in Medina until his death. He is one of the most prolific narrators of hadith, transmitting thousands of reports about the Prophet's words, actions, and character. The Prophet prayed for his long life, abundant wealth, and offspring, and Anas lived to approximately one hundred years, becoming a major source of knowledge for the early Muslim community.
rafi-bin-khadij-ra: Rafi bin Khadij, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Ansar of Medina. He participated in many of the Prophet's military campaigns and narrated numerous hadiths covering agricultural practices, business transactions, and acts of worship. He is particularly known for narrating the hadith prohibiting certain forms of land rental arrangements. His reports are preserved in the major hadith collections.
ali-bin-abi-talib-ra: Ali bin Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with him, was the cousin and son-in-law of the Prophet, having married his daughter Fatimah. He was among the first to accept Islam and was raised in the Prophet's household. He served as the fourth Rightly Guided Caliph and is one of the most important narrators of hadith, transmitting hundreds of the Prophet's statements and practices. He was renowned for his knowledge, courage, and piety.
amr-bin-shuaib-on-his-fathers-authority-: This chain refers to Amr bin Shu'aib narrating on the authority of his father Shu'aib, who narrated from his grandfather Abdullah bin Amr bin al-As, may Allah be pleased with him. Abdullah bin Amr was a prolific companion who was permitted by the Prophet to write down hadiths, and he compiled an early collection called al-Sahifah al-Sadiqah. This three-link chain (Amr from his father from his grandfather) is a well-recognized isnad in hadith literature, with scholars debating its precise degree of reliability.

This entry refers to the transmission chain of Amr bin Shu'aib, narrating from his father Shu'aib ibn Muhammad, who narrated from his grandfather Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-As, may Allah be pleased with him. Abdullah ibn Amr was among the most dedicated companions in recording the Prophet's sayings with his written notes. The chain, though sometimes debated by hadith specialists regarding whether Shu'aib had direct contact with his grandfather, is accepted by the majority of scholars and appears frequently in the Sunan collections.
from-abu-hurairah: Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with him, was the most prolific narrator of hadith among all the companions, transmitting over five thousand reports from the Prophet. He embraced Islam in the seventh year of the Hijrah and dedicated himself entirely to accompanying the Prophet and memorizing his teachings. The Prophet testified to his keen memory, and he became the foremost transmitter of the Prophetic Sunnah. His narrations form the backbone of the major hadith collections.
it-was-narrated-that-abu-hurairah-said: Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the Ahl al-Suffah, the poor companions who lived in the mosque, and the most prolific transmitter of hadith. He memorized an enormous number of the Prophet's sayings and supplicated to Allah to strengthen his memory, which the Prophet confirmed with a gesture. Thousands of narrations from him are preserved in the major collections, making him an indispensable source for knowledge of the Sunnah.
ibn-buraidah: Ibn Buraidah refers to the sons of Buraidah ibn al-Husayb al-Aslami, may Allah be pleased with him — most commonly Abdullah ibn Buraidah or Sulaiman ibn Buraidah, both of whom were Tabi'in narrators. They transmitted hadiths from their father Buraidah, a companion of the Prophet, as well as from other companions. Their narrations are found in the major hadith collections across a range of topics including prayer, leadership, and personal conduct.
al-mughirah-bin-shubah: Al-Mughirah bin Shu'bah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet renowned for his intelligence and political acumen, such that he was described as the most cunning of the Arabs. He embraced Islam before the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah and went on to serve as a governor and military commander. He narrated numerous hadiths from the Prophet and was one of the last companions to die, passing away around 50 AH.
abu-ishaq: Abu Ishaq al-Sabi'i (Amr ibn Abdullah al-Hamdani) was a highly prolific Tabi'i narrator from Kufa who transmitted hadiths from a large number of companions and senior Tabi'in. He is one of the most frequently cited narrators in the hadith collections and was known for his vast knowledge, though later in life his memory weakened somewhat. Scholars of hadith praised him highly and his narrations appear in all the major collections.
shubah: Shu'bah ibn al-Hajjaj was one of the most celebrated hadith critics and narrators of the second generation of Muslims (Tabi'in and their successors). He was among the first to systematically scrutinize the reliability of hadith narrators and is considered a founding figure of the science of hadith criticism. His narrations and evaluations of other narrators are foundational references in the major hadith collections.
salim-and-hamzah-the-sons-of-abdullah-bi: Salim and Hamzah were two sons of the great companion Abdullah ibn Umar, may Allah be pleased with him. Salim ibn Abdullah was an eminent Tabi'i scholar and jurist, considered one of the seven great jurists of Medina, who transmitted extensively from his father. Hamzah ibn Abdullah also narrated hadiths from his father. Together they represent an important link in transmitting the Sunnah from the household of Umar ibn al-Khattab to subsequent generations.
musa-bin-talhah: Musa bin Talhah was the son of the companion Talhah ibn Ubaydullah, may Allah be pleased with him, one of the ten companions promised Paradise. He was a Tabi'i narrator who transmitted hadiths from his father and from other companions. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections and he was regarded as a reliable transmitter. He lived in Kufa and was known for his scholarship and piety.
abu-hurairah-may-allah-be-pleased-with-h: Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion who spent three years in close companionship with the Prophet, dedicating himself to memorizing and preserving the Prophet's teachings. He is the single most prolific narrator of hadith, with over five thousand narrations attributed to him in the major collections. The Prophet himself acknowledged Abu Hurairah's extraordinary memory when he spread his cloak and then told him to gather it to his chest, after which Abu Hurairah said he never forgot anything.
aishah: A'ishah bint Abi Bakr, may Allah be pleased with her, was the beloved wife of the Prophet and daughter of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, the first caliph. She is one of the most prolific narrators of hadith and one of the greatest scholars the Muslim community has ever known. She narrated approximately two thousand hadiths and was a primary authority on matters of personal conduct, worship, and the Prophet's private life. Companions and Tabi'in came to her for religious rulings throughout her life.
abu-qatadah: Abu Qatadah al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Ansar of Medina, renowned as one of the Prophet's finest horsemen and a brave fighter in the early Islamic battles. He narrated numerous hadiths from the Prophet on topics including prayer, fasting, and general conduct. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections, and he was held in high esteem by the companions for his courage and devotion.
abu-khaldah: Abu Khaldah Khalid ibn Dinar al-Ataki was a Tabi'i narrator from Basra who transmitted hadiths from companions and senior Tabi'in. He is mentioned in the chains of transmission of certain hadiths found in the Sunan collections. Scholars of hadith regarded him as an acceptable narrator, and his narrations provide testimony to the religious practices and teachings of the early Muslim community.
al-miqdad-ibn-al-aswad: Al-Miqdad ibn al-Aswad, may Allah be pleased with him, was among the earliest companions to embrace Islam and one of the first horsemen in the Muslim army. His actual lineage was to Amr ibn Tha'labah, but he was adopted by al-Aswad ibn Abd Yaghuth, hence the name Ibn al-Aswad. He participated in the Battle of Badr and other major campaigns and narrated hadiths from the Prophet. He was known for his bravery and steadfastness in the face of persecution before the Hijrah.
abuayyub: AbuAyyub al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, was the companion who hosted the Prophet when he first arrived in Medina, an honor that brought him great distinction. His full name was Khalid ibn Zayd ibn Kulayb, and he was from the Banu Najjar clan of the Ansar. He participated in virtually all of the Prophet's campaigns and continued to participate in Islamic military expeditions well into old age, eventually dying near Constantinople while on campaign, where his grave remains a site of reverence.
uthman-ibn-abulas: Uthman ibn Abul-As al-Thaqafi, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet appointed by him as the governor of Taif. He is notable for asking the Prophet to teach him to lead his people in prayer and for the Prophet's advice to him about keeping prayers short and accessible to the congregation. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and was a respected figure among the companions for his knowledge and administrative service.
kab-ibn-ujrah: Ka'b ibn Ujrah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet and the narrator of the famous hadith about the fidyah (ransom) for shaving the head during ihram due to illness. He also narrated the complete form of the Abrahamic prayer upon the Prophet, which forms part of the tashahhud recited in every prayer. He lived in Medina and later in Kufa, and his narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections.
abdullah-ibn-as-saib: Abdullah ibn as-Sa'ib, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet and a noted reciter of the Quran. He was appointed as a teacher of Quranic recitation in Mecca and was among those who narrated hadiths from the Prophet. His narrations relating to prayer and Quranic recitation are preserved in the Sunan collections, and he was regarded as an authority on the proper recitation of the Quran.
abumalik-al-ashari: AbuMalik al-Ash'ari, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Ash'ari tribe of Yemen. He is the narrator of the famous hadith beginning 'Purity is half of faith,' which enumerates the virtues of various acts of worship. His narrations are preserved in Sahih Muslim and other major collections, and he was esteemed among the companions for his piety and knowledge of the Prophet's teachings.
rifaah-ibn-rafi: Rifa'ah ibn Rafi al-Ansari al-Zuraqi, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Ansar of Medina and participated in the Battle of Badr. He narrated several hadiths from the Prophet, including narrations about prayer and its proper performance. His narrations are preserved in the Sunan collections, and he was respected among the companions for his early acceptance of Islam and his participation in the foundational battles.
uthman-ibn-affan: Uthman ibn Affan, may Allah be pleased with him, was the third Rightly Guided Caliph and one of the most beloved companions of the Prophet. He was married to two of the Prophet's daughters in succession, earning him the title Dhul-Nurayn (possessor of two lights). He narrated many hadiths from the Prophet and is particularly associated with narrations about the virtues of Wudu and prayer. He compiled the standardized written Quran during his caliphate, an act of immense service to Islam.
abdullah-ibn-abuawfa: Abdullah ibn AbuAwfa al-Aslami, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Aslam tribe who participated in the pledge of Ridwan under the tree at Hudaybiyyah. He lived to a very advanced age and was among the last companions to die in Kufa, making him a living link to the Prophet for the people of Iraq. He narrated numerous hadiths and was a major source of Prophetic knowledge for the Tabi'in of Kufa.
sahl-ibn-al-hanzaliyyah: Sahl ibn al-Hanzaliyyah al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who participated in the pledge of Ridwan at Hudaybiyyah. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and was known for his piety and dedication to worship. His narrations are found in the Sunan collections and touch upon topics of vigilance, guarding posts, and the ethics of Islamic conduct.
abdullah-ibn-az-zubayr: Abdullah ibn az-Zubayr, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of the companion Zubayr ibn al-Awwam and the grandson of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq through his mother Asma'. He was the first child born to the Muhajirun in Medina after the Hijrah, which was seen as a blessed sign. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and from his father and aunt A'ishah. He later became caliph and ruled from Mecca for several years, known for his asceticism and bravery.
ata-ibn-yasar: Ata ibn Yasar was a distinguished Tabi'i scholar based in Medina who narrated from numerous companions including Abu Hurairah, Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, and A'ishah, may Allah be pleased with them. He was among the most reliable and knowledgeable of the Tabi'in and was particularly noted for his narrations about worship and ethics. His hadith appear in all the major collections, and he was praised by scholars of hadith for his precision and piety.
abdullah-ibn-jafar: Abdullah ibn Ja'far, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, the Prophet's cousin who died as a martyr at the Battle of Mu'tah. He grew up in the Prophet's household after his father's martyrdom and was personally known to the Prophet, who prayed for blessings upon him. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and from other companions, and was known for his generosity, a trait inherited from his father.
abd-al-rahman-b-samurah: Abd al-Rahman ibn Samurah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who participated in the Islamic conquests and served as a military commander in Sijistan and Khorasan. The Prophet gave him notable advice not to seek positions of leadership and not to take oaths lightly. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections and reflect important guidance from the Prophet on leadership and personal conduct.
muadh-ibn-anas-al-juhani: Mu'adh ibn Anas al-Juhani, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Juhainah tribe who settled in Egypt after the Islamic conquests. He narrated several hadiths from the Prophet relating to prayer, supplication, and good manners. His narrations are preserved in the Sunan collections, particularly the Sunan of Abu Dawud, and reflect the practical religious guidance the Prophet offered to his companions.
abd-allah-b-masud: Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, may Allah be pleased with him, was among the first to embrace Islam and one of the greatest scholars among the companions. He served as the Prophet's personal attendant and was so close to him that the companions considered him part of the Prophet's household. He narrated a large number of hadiths and was the leading religious authority in Kufa during the early caliphate period. He was particularly renowned for his recitation and knowledge of the Quran.
abuayyub-al-ansari: AbuAyyub al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, whose full name was Khalid ibn Zayd, was the companion who had the honor of hosting the Prophet upon his arrival in Medina. The Prophet stayed in his home for several months before the construction of his mosque and residence was completed. He participated in numerous battles and continued to join military expeditions until old age, dying near Constantinople around 52 AH. His narrations from the Prophet are preserved in the major hadith collections.
jabir-ibn-atik: Jabir ibn Atik al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Ansar of Medina. He is known as the narrator of the hadith about the deaths that are considered martyrdom beyond dying in battle, including dying of plague, drowning, and other causes. He participated in the early battles alongside the Prophet, and his narrations are preserved in the Sunan collections.
awf-ibn-malik: Awf ibn Malik al-Ashja'i, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who participated in the Islamic conquests and settled in Syria. He narrated numerous hadiths from the Prophet on topics including prayer, leadership, supplication, and eschatology. He is known for narrating the famous hadith describing the signs of the Hour and the qualities of good and bad leaders. His narrations are found across the major hadith collections.
yala-ibn-umayyah: Ya'la ibn Umayyah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet also known as Ya'la ibn Munabbih al-Tamimi. He narrated several hadiths from the Prophet relating to the rites of Hajj and the rules of ihram. He is known for narrating the account of a man who came to the Prophet asking about performing Hajj while wearing a perfumed garment, and the Prophet's guidance in that situation. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections.
ikrimah: Ikrimah was a prominent Tabi'i scholar and the freed slave of Abdullah ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with him. He is one of the most important transmitters of knowledge from Ibn Abbas and spent years studying under him, becoming one of the foremost authorities on Quranic exegesis and hadith. He traveled widely throughout the Muslim world transmitting knowledge and is cited extensively in the tafsir and hadith literature, though some classical scholars debated aspects of his reliability.
adi-ibn-hatim: Adi ibn Hatim al-Ta'i, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of the celebrated pre-Islamic Arab leader Hatim al-Ta'i, famous for his generosity. Adi was initially a Christian before embracing Islam and visiting the Prophet, and he went on to become a devoted companion and a prolific narrator of hadith. He was among those who remained steadfast during the apostasy wars and lived to a very advanced age, narrating from the Prophet about matters of faith, hunting, and lawful food.
umm-kurz: Umm Kurz al-Ka'biyyah, may Allah be pleased with her, was a companion of the Prophet from Mecca who narrated hadiths relating to the aqiqah (the sacrifice made for a newborn). She is particularly known for transmitting the Prophet's guidance on the number of animals to be slaughtered for a newborn boy versus a girl. Her narrations are preserved in the Sunan collections and represent an important source for rulings related to the aqiqah.
jubair-b-mutim: Jubayr ibn Mut'im, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Quraysh tribe and a recognized authority on genealogy and tribal lineages. He embraced Islam after the conquest of Mecca. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet on theology, prayer, and the attributes of Allah, including the famous narration of the Prophet reciting Surah al-Tur in prayer. His narrations are preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and the Sunan collections.
abyad-ibn-hammal: Abyad ibn Hammal al-Ma'ribi, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who came from the Yemen region. He is particularly known for narrating hadiths about the iqta (land grants) made by the Prophet and for a notable exchange with the Prophet regarding the salt mines of Ma'rib. His narrations are preserved in the Sunan collections, particularly those of Abu Dawud and al-Tirmidhi.
abubarzah-al-aslami: AbuBarzah al-Aslami, may Allah be pleased with him, whose full name was Nadlah ibn Ubayd, was a companion of the Prophet from the Aslam tribe. He participated in several of the Prophet's military campaigns and was among those who accompanied the Prophet during the Hudaybiyyah expedition. He narrated numerous hadiths on prayer times, personal conduct, and piety, and lived a long life transmitting the Prophet's teachings to later generations.
thabit-bin-adh-dahhak: Thabit bin Adh-Dahhak al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who gave the pledge of allegiance at Hudaybiyyah under the tree. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and is known for transmitting important reports on vows, oaths, and matters of faith. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections including Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.
zaid-b-thabit: Zayd ibn Thabit, may Allah be pleased with him, was the primary scribe of the Prophet's revelations and one of the most learned companions in knowledge of the Quran and its rulings on inheritance. He was entrusted by Abu Bakr al-Siddiq and later Uthman ibn Affan with the monumental task of compiling the written Quran. He also narrated many hadiths from the Prophet and was considered a supreme authority in Medina on matters of law and Quranic sciences.
hakim-ibn-hizam: Hakim ibn Hizam, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet and the nephew of Khadijah, the Prophet's first wife. He embraced Islam at the conquest of Mecca and narrated hadiths from the Prophet on trade ethics, transactions, and generosity. He was known for his noble character and generosity even before Islam. He lived to approximately one hundred and twenty years and was a respected narrator whose reports appear in the major collections.
hudhayfah-ibn-al-yaman: Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who was entrusted with the Prophet's secrets regarding the hypocrites (munafiqin), earning him the title 'keeper of the Prophet's secret.' He was a major narrator of hadiths concerning the trials and tribulations (fitan) of the end times and the signs of the Hour. His narrations on eschatology are among the most important in the hadith collections, and he served as a governor under Umar ibn al-Khattab.
abu-qilabah: Abu Qilabah al-Jarmi (Abdullah ibn Zayd) was a distinguished Tabi'i scholar from Basra who narrated hadiths from numerous companions. He was known for his piety, his avoidance of sitting with rulers, and his extensive travels in search of hadith. He is cited in all the major collections and was regarded by hadith scholars as a reliable and precise narrator. His narrations cover a broad range of Islamic jurisprudence and ethics.
al-miswar-b-makhramah: Al-Miswar ibn Makhramah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who was brought to Medina as a young child and grew up there. He was the nephew of Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf, one of the ten companions promised Paradise. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and from senior companions and was an important narrator in Medina, known for his accurate transmission and his opposition to certain political decisions after the Prophet's death.
salamah-ibn-al-muhabbaq: Salamah ibn al-Muhabbaq al-Hudhali, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who settled in Basra after the Islamic conquests. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet on matters of personal conduct and legal rulings. His narrations are found in the Sunan collections and he is known to hadith scholars as a companion narrator whose reports reflect the practical teachings of the Prophet.
abu-talhah: Abu Talhah al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, whose name was Zayd ibn Sahl, was a companion of the Prophet and one of the greatest archers among the Ansar. He was known for his bravery, generosity, and love for the Prophet, famously offering his own body as a shield for the Prophet at the Battle of Uhud. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and is known for the hadith about his garden, Bayraha, which he donated in charity. His narrations are preserved in the major collections.
abumusa: AbuMusa al-Ash'ari, may Allah be pleased with him, whose full name was Abdullah ibn Qays, was a companion of the Prophet from Yemen and one of the most distinguished scholars and governors of the early Islamic period. He was appointed governor of Kufa and Basra by Umar ibn al-Khattab and was renowned for his melodious Quran recitation, which the Prophet praised by saying his voice was like a flute of the family of David. He narrated numerous hadiths and was a key religious authority in Iraq.
abdullah-bin-ubaidullah-bin-abi-mulaika: Abdullah bin Ubaidullah bin Abi Mulaika was a Tabi'i narrator from Mecca who transmitted hadiths from numerous companions and had access to the major companions of Mecca and Medina. He served as a judge in Mecca and was considered a reliable authority in hadith. His narrations appear in the major collections and span various topics of Islamic jurisprudence and practice.
abdur-rahman-bin-abi-laila: Abdur-Rahman bin Abi Laila was a distinguished Tabi'i jurist and narrator from Kufa who transmitted hadiths from a large number of companions. He is particularly known for narrating the hadith about Ka'b ibn Ujrah and the complete form of the prayer upon the Prophet (salawat). He was a judge in Kufa under Ali ibn Abi Talib and later under subsequent governors, and his narrations are found in all the major hadith collections.
muhammad-bin-abi-al-mujalid: Muhammad bin Abi Al-Mujalid was a Tabi'i narrator who transmitted hadiths from senior Tabi'in and occasionally from companions. He is found in the chains of transmission of certain hadiths in the Sunan collections. Scholars of hadith accepted his narrations, and his reports contribute to the record of the Prophet's guidance as transmitted through the early Muslim community.
rabi-bin-hirash: Rabi bin Hirash al-Absi was a distinguished Tabi'i scholar from Kufa renowned for his scrupulousness and piety. He narrated hadiths from Ali ibn Abi Talib, Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman, and other companions. He was known for never laughing after witnessing the washing of a deceased person, reflecting his deep awareness of death. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections and he was highly regarded by scholars of hadith for his reliability.
abu-waqid-al-laithi: Abu Waqid al-Laithi, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Layth tribe. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet on matters including the slaughter of animals, supplication, and Islamic conduct. He is known for the narration about the prohibition of cutting meat from a living animal. His narrations are preserved in the Sunan collections and the Muwatta of Imam Malik.
abu-burdas-father: This entry refers to Abu Musa al-Ash'ari, may Allah be pleased with him, who is the father of Abu Burda ibn Abi Musa. Abu Musa was a companion of the Prophet from Yemen, renowned for his Quranic recitation, his scholarship, and his service as governor of Kufa and Basra under Umar ibn al-Khattab. He is one of the major narrators among the companions, and hadiths transmitted through his son Abu Burda from him form an important strand of the hadith literature.
humran: Humran ibn Aban was the freed slave of Uthman ibn Affan, may Allah be pleased with him, and a major Tabi'i narrator of hadiths from Uthman. He is particularly known for transmitting Uthman's detailed narrations about the proper performance of wudu (ablution) and the virtues of prayer. His narrations are found in Sahih Muslim and the Sunan collections, and he is regarded as a reliable and important link in the chain transmitting Uthman's knowledge to later generations.
um-atiya: Umm Atiyyah al-Ansariyyah, may Allah be pleased with her, was a companion of the Prophet from the Ansar of Medina. She participated in several of the Prophet's military campaigns in the role of nursing the wounded and caring for the sick. She is a primary narrator of hadiths relating to the ritual washing of the deceased, menstruation, and other matters of particular relevance to women. Her narrations are preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and the Sunan collections.
